skill.name: gws-forms
skill.description: Use the gws CLI to inspect, create, read, update, publish, and manage Google Forms resources.
skill.summary: Manage Google Forms through documented gws CLI commands.
skill.author: googleworkspace

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks as shell execution, but the reviewed file uses Markdown code spans and fenced CLI examples. The real risks are expected gws command execution against Google Forms and a sibling shared-skill reference on line 16; no malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: External Google Workspace CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use gws forms and gws schema commands. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can read or modify Google Forms data using the active account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly documents CLI commands that operate on Google Forms. The commands are expected for this skill, so the concern is operational impact rather than command injection.

medium_findings.title: Relative Shared Skill Dependency
medium_findings.description: Line 16 tells the assistant to read ../gws-shared/SKILL.md and run gws generate-skills if it is missing. This parent-directory reference is not malicious by itself, but it expands trusted context outside this skill file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory reference is explicit and relevant to authentication rules. The risk is moderate because this may be normal shared documentation, but reviewers should verify the referenced file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown command examples and inline command names. No Ruby code or dynamic shell interpolation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confidence that this is dangerous execution is very low because the evidence is Markdown documentation. The examples still represent commands an assistant may run.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Patterns Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits at lines 3 and 27 appear in ordinary prose about Google Forms. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confidence that the weak crypto warning is a real security issue is extremely low. The lines contain descriptive text, not cryptographic implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI Commands Affecting Workspace Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides gws command patterns for Forms methods. These commands should be treated as data-affecting operations and reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are clear and directly tied to Google Forms operations. The pattern is risky only when used without user confirmation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parent Directory Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The prerequisite points to ../gws-shared/SKILL.md. Parent-directory references can import instructions outside the reviewed skill package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal sequence is present and intentional. It appears to support shared authentication guidance, so it is not enough to block publication.

content.value_statement: Google Forms administration can require exact API methods and carefully built parameters. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through gws commands for Forms resources.
actual_capabilities: Shows the gws forms command pattern for resource and method calls.
Lists supported Forms API resources, including forms, responses, and watches.
Documents form creation behavior and its title-only limitations.
Guides users to inspect method schemas before building parameters.
Supports workflows for reading forms and applying batch updates.
Mentions publish settings updates for supported modern forms.
limitations: Requires the gws CLI and working Google Workspace authentication.
Relies on a shared gws skill for authentication and security rules.
Does not include complete parameter examples for each Forms method.
Cannot bypass Google API permissions or legacy Forms limitations.

use_cases.title: Create a New Form
use_cases.description: Create an empty Google Form with a title, then plan follow-up updates for questions and settings.

use_cases.title: Inspect Existing Forms
use_cases.description: Retrieve form details and verify available resources before making updates through the gws CLI.

use_cases.title: Automate Form Maintenance
use_cases.description: Use schema inspection and batch updates to support repeatable form changes across team workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Form Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to inspect available gws Forms resources and methods before I run any command.

prompt_templates.title: Read a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me retrieve an existing Google Form after checking the required schema and parameters.

prompt_templates.title: Create and Populate a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan the gws steps to create a titled form, then add items with a follow-up update operation.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Batch Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my intended Google Forms batch update, verify required parameters, and flag risky changes before execution.

output_examples.input: Inspect the Forms create method before building a command.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the method schema to check, explains the title-only create behavior, and asks for the desired form title.

output_examples.input: Prepare to update publish settings for a form.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the form must support publish settings, checks the method requirements, and summarizes the change for approval.

output_examples.input: Find available operations for form responses.
output_examples.output: The assistant points to the responses resource and recommends schema inspection before selecting a method.

best_practices: Inspect the method schema before building parameters for any Forms operation.
Confirm the active Google account and target form before running write operations.
Create a form first, then add items through a separate update operation.
anti_patterns: Running write operations without reviewing the resolved parameters.
Assuming forms.create can copy items, settings, or descriptions.
Using this skill without installed gws authentication and shared security guidance.

faq.question: Does this skill require the gws CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents gws commands and depends on the gws CLI being installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it create a complete form in one step?
faq.answer: No. The skill notes that creation only copies title fields. Add items with a later update.

faq.question: Can it read form responses?
faq.answer: It lists the responses resource and directs users to inspect available methods with the schema command.

faq.question: Does it manage publish settings?
faq.answer: Yes, it identifies setPublishSettings for supported forms. Legacy forms without publish settings are not supported.

faq.question: Is this safe for production forms?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review schemas, parameters, account context, and user approval before running any write operation.

faq.question: Does it include authentication instructions?
faq.answer: It points to a shared gws skill for authentication, global flags, and security rules.
